concepts.title: Buffer Solution and types of Buffer
concepts.conceptBody:
BUFFERS

Certain solutions, such as that of ammonium acetate, have a tendency to resist any change in its hydronium ion concentration or $\text{pH}$, whenever a small amount of a strong acid or a strong base is added to it. This property of a solution is known as buffer action.

Buffer Solution

A solution which resists any change of $\text{pH}$ when a small amount of a strong acid or a strong base is added to it, is called a buffer solution or simply as a buffer.

Alternatively, a buffer solution may be defined as a solution whose $\text{pH}$ value does not change appreciatbly upon the addition of small amounts of a strong acid, base and/or water from outside. Thus, buffers have reserve acidity and reserve alkalinity.

Buffer solutions usually consist of a mixture of a weak acid and its salt with a strong base e.g., $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COONa}$, or that of a weak base and its salt with a strong acid e.g., $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$. The solution of any salt of a weak acid and weak base e.g., ammonium acetate, also shows buffering property.

Types of Buffers

There are two types of buffers, acid buffer and basic buffer.

Acid buffer

A buffer solution containing a large amounts of a weak acid, and its salt with a strong base, is termed as an acid buffer. Such buffer solutions have $\text{pH}$ on the acidic side i.e., $\text{pH}$ is less than 7 at $298~\text{K}$. The $\text{pH}$ of an acid buffer is given by the equation.

$\text{pH}=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}+\log \frac{[\text{ salt }]}{[\text{ acid }]}$

where ${{K}_{a}}$ is the acid dissociation constant of the weak acid.

Basic buffer

A buffer solution containing relatively large amounts of a weak base and its salt with a strong acid, it termed as a basic buffer. Such buffers have $\text{pH}$ on the alkaline side i.e., $\text{pH}$ is higher than 7 at $298~\text{K}$.

The $\text{pH}$ of a basic buffer is given by the equation:

$\text{pOH}=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}+\log \frac{[\text{ salt }]}{[\text{ base }]}$

where ${{K}_{b}}$ is the base dissociation constant of the weak base.

These equations are called Henderson equation .

concepts.title: Buffer Capacity and Buffer Range
concepts.conceptBody:
Buffer-capacity and Buffer-range

The effectiveness of any buffer is described in terms of its buffer capacity. It is defined as, the number of equivalents of a strong acid (or a strong base) required to change the $\text{pH}$ of one litre of a buffer solution by one unit, keeping the total amount of the acid and the salt in the buffer constant’.

The buffer capacity of a buffer in maximum when acid to salt or base to salt ratio is equal to 1 i.e. it contains equal number of moles of acid (or base) and the salt. All buffer solutions remain effective over a small $\text{pH}$ range: this $\text{pH}$-range is characteristic of the buffer and is termed as the buffer-range.

For the two types of buffers, it is given by

Buffer range in $\text{pH}$ units

Acid buffer: $\quad \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}-1\quad $ to $\quad \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}+1$

Basic buffer: $\quad \left( \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}}-\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \right)-1$ to $\left. \left( \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \right)-\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}} \right)+1$

Consider acetic acid - sodium acetate buffer, an acid buffer. the acid dissociation constant $\left( {{K}_{a}} \right)$ of acetic acid is $1.84\quad {{10}^{-5}}$.

Therefore, $\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ for acetic acid is 4.74. Then, the buffer range of an acetic acid - sodium acetate buffer is,

$\text{pH}=\left( \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} \right)-1\text{ to }\left( \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}} \right)+1$

$=4.74-1\text{ to }4.74+1$

$=3.74\text{ to }5.74$

Thus, the acetic acid - sodium acetate buffer will act as an effective buffer over the pH range 3.74 to 5.74.

The $\text{pH}$ of a buffer solution depends only on the ratio of the concentrations of the salt and the acid, or salt and the base. It does not depend on the individual concentration. Since, the ratio remains the same even when the solution is diluted. However, at high dilutions, buffers do not remain effective as, they are not able to resist a change in the $\text{pH}$ value due to the addition of a strong acid or a strong base.

concepts.title: Acid-Base indicators
concepts.conceptBody:
Acid-Base Indicators

The amount of an acid (or a base), which is exactly equivalent chemically to the amount of some standard base (or an acid), is determined by an acid-base titration. The point of equivalence is called end point. The solution of a strong acid and strong base will be neutral at the end point and have a $\text{pH}$ of 7 as they are strong electrolytes. However, if either the acid or the base is a weak electrolyte, the solution at the equivalence point will be either slightly alkaline $(\text{pH}>7)$ or slightly acidic $(\text{pH}<7)$. Hence, for an acid-base titration, the correct end point will be characterized by a definite value of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ion concentration (i.e. $\text{pH}$), which depends upon the nature of the acid and the base and the concentration in the solution.

A large number of acid-base indicators are available which possess different colours according to ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ion concentration of the solution. These indicators do not change from predominantly ‘acid’ colour to predominantly ‘base’ suddently and abruptly, but within a small interval of $\text{pH}$ (generally about $2\text{pH}$ units). This is called colour-charge interval of the indicator. The position of the colourcharge interval in the $\text{pH}$ scale varies widely with different indicators. Therefore, for acid base titrations, an indicator is chosen which shoes a distinct colour change at a $\text{pH}$ close to that of equivalent point.

concepts.title: Acid-Base indicator behaviour
concepts.conceptBody:
Acid-base indicator behaviour

The mechanism of acid-base indicator was developed by Ostwald, which offered an explanation for the colour change with change in $\text{pH}$. All acid-base indicators are weak organic acids or bases, which posses different colours in unionized and ionized states. If the indicator acid is represented as HIn and has its ionized form in, then equilibrium in aqueous solution may be represented as:

\[\underset{\begin{smallmatrix}

\text{Unionized} \\

\text{ colour}

\end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{H\ln }}\,\rightleftharpoons \underset{\begin{smallmatrix}

\text{Ionized} \\

\text{ colour}

\end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{{{H}^{+}}+I{{n}^{-}}}}\,\]

For a basic indicator,

$\underset{\begin{smallmatrix}

\text{Unionised} \\

\text{ colour}

\end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{\text{InOH}}}\,\rightleftharpoons \underset{\begin{smallmatrix}

\text{Ionised} \\

\text{ colour}

\end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+\text{I}{{\text{n}}^{+}}}}\,$

The unionized molecule has one colour while the ionized ions has another colour.

The equilibrium constant for a weak acid indicator may be written as:

${{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind }}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HIn}]}$

where, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind }}}$ is known as indicator constant.

$\text{ or }\quad \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\frac{[\text{HIn}]}{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{-}} \right]}\times {{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind }}}$

Writing the above equation in logarithmic form

\[\log \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\log \frac{[\text{HIn}]}{\left[ {{\operatorname{In}}^{-}} \right]}\times \text{KInd }\]

or \[-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=-\log \text{KInd}+\log \frac{\left[ {{\operatorname{In}}^{+}} \right]}{[\text{HIn}]}\,\,\,\,(\because \text{pX}=-\log \text{X})\]

Thus the colour of the indicator, which is determined by the ratio

$\frac{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HIn}]}$ is governed by the $\text{pH}$ of the solution,

Similarly, for a weak organic base indicator, In $\text{OH}$,

$\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]=\frac{[\text{InOH}]}{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{+}} \right]}\times \text{KInd}$

or $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\frac{\text{Kw}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind}}}}\cdot \frac{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{+}} \right]}{[\text{InOH}]}$

Taking logarithms

$\log \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\log \frac{\text{Kw}}{{{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind}}}}+\log \frac{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{+}} \right]}{[\text{InOH}]}\text{ }$

or $-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=-\log \text{Kw}+\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{In}}}+\log \frac{[\text{InOH}]}{\left[ {{\operatorname{In}}^{+}} \right]}$

$\therefore \quad \text{pH}=\text{pKw}-\text{pKI}{{\text{n}}_{\text{In}}}+\log \frac{[\text{InOH}]}{\left[ {{\operatorname{In}}^{+}} \right]}$

or \[\text{pH}=14-\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{In}}}+\log \frac{[\text{InOH}]}{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{+}} \right]}\]at ${{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C}$

As the $\text{pH}$ of the solution changes the colour of the indictor changes. This change is so minute that it goes unnoticed by the human eye. therefore to see the colour change the concentration of one of the forms should predominate. The solution will have the acid colour (i.e. of HIn form) when the ratio $[\text{HIn}]$ to $[\text{In}]$ is approximately 10 and the alkaline colour (i.e. of $\text{I}{{\text{n}}^{-}}$) when ratio [In-] to [HIn] is approximately 10.

The acid colour will be visible when

$\frac{[\text{HIn}]}{\left[ \text{I}{{\text{n}}^{-}} \right]}\ge 10$

Only basic colour will be visible when

$\frac{\left[ {{\operatorname{In}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HIn}]}\ge 10$

Hence the colour interval or indicator $\text{pH}$ range is

Indicator $\text{pH}$ range $=-1+\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind }}}$ to $1+\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind }}}$

Indicator $\text{pH}$ range $=\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{Ind }}}\pm 1$

This means that the colour change interval of an indictor lies over approximately $2\text{pH}$ units. Within this range, the change from one colour to the other another will be visible to human eye.

concepts.title: Acid-Base Titration Curves
concepts.conceptBody:
Acid Base Titration Curves

In acid base titrations, a base is added to an acid successively (or vice versa) and the $\text{pH}$ of the solution is noted after every addition. the plot of $\text{pH}$ against the amount of base (or acid) added is called a titration curve. The shape of the curve depends upon the ionization constants of the acid and base used in the titration.

(1) Titration of strong acid and strong base

When we add a solution of $\text{NaOH}$ to the solution of $\text{HCl}$, the $\text{pH}$ progressively increases.

$\text{HCl}(\text{aq})+\text{NaOH}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{NaCl}(\text{aq})+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

This is because $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions from the base will react with ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions of the acid to form water. This decreases the concentration of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions and therefore $\text{pH}$ increases. The $\text{pH}$ of the solution increases only slightly in the beginning until just before the stoichiometric point, there is a sudden jump in $\text{pH}$. The $\text{pH}$ continues to increase after the stoichiometric point but then levels off because of the presence of excess strong base in the solution. At the equivalence point the $\text{pH}$ is 7 , but has increased sharply from 3.5 to 11 just before this point. Any indicator, which has effective range between $\text{pH}3.5$ to 11 may be used to detect the equivalence point. For example, phenolphthalein and methyl orange are good indicators in this range.

When we tirtate the base $(\text{NaOH})$ with the continuous addition of acid $(\text{HCl})$, the curve obtained is exactly reversed. The $\text{pH}$ decreases slowly in the beginning and near the equivalence point there is sudden fall through $\text{pH}=7$ as the concentration of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions increases sharply.

(2) Titration of weak acid and a strong base

This type of titration is carried out between acetic acid and sodium hydroxide.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO}{{\text{H}}_{(\text{aq})}}+\text{NaOH}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COON}{{\text{a}}_{(\text{aq})}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

The free ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ion from the weak acid is neutralized by $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions from the base and there is a small increase in $\text{pH}$. Around the equivalence point large increase in $\text{pH}$ is observed. However, the slope of the curve is not as sleep as in the case of strong base and strong acid titration curve. Sodium $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions of water (hydrolysis) and so at the equivalence point the $\text{pH}$ is not 7 . The ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ion concentration decreases and the $\text{pH}$ is about 8.7 at the equivalence point. By the addition of small amount of $\text{NaOH},\text{pH}$ change is observed from about 8.0 to 10.0 near the equivalence point. It is therefore necessary to use an indicator with $\text{pH}$ range on alkaline side. Phenolphthalein is commonly used. Methyl orange cannot be used because it would give inaccurate indication of equivalence point.

The $\text{pH}$ titration curve of weak acid $\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)$ and strong base $(\text{NaOH})$

(3) Titration of weak base and a strong acid

This type of titration is carried out between a weak base such as ammonium hydroxide and strong acid such as hydrochloric acid.

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{O}{{\text{H}}_{(\text{aq})}}+\text{HC}{{\text{l}}_{(\text{aq})}}\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{(\text{aq})}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

The equivalence point is below 7 because the salt $\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl} \right)$ formed at the neutralization reacts with water to give ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions. The equivalence point lies at about $\text{pH}$ 5.3. It is, therefore necessary to use an indicator with $\text{pH}$ range slightly on the acidic side. Methyl orange can be used. Phenolphthalein is not suitable because its colour change occurs away from the equivalence point.

The $\text{pH}$ titration curve of weak base $\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH} \right)$ and strong acid ($\text{HCl})$

(4) Titration of weak acid and weak base

This type of titration is carried out between a weak acid such as acetic acid $\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)$ and weak base such as ammonium hydroxide $\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH} \right)$.

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{O}{{\text{H}}_{(\text{aq})}}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO}{{\text{H}}_{(\text{aq})}}\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COON}{{\text{H}}_{4(\text{aq})}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

There is no sharp change in the $\text{pH}$ during the titration. Hence, no sharp equivalence point can be obtained with common indicators. However, a mixed indicator, which shows a sharp colour change over limited $\text{pH}$ range may be used.
